question: Ship ask-the-board as one open-source BYOK engine with an inbound-only (pull) go-to-market -- free product, adoption first, monetize later via a capped hosted tier -- or run an outbound motion to seed demand?
prior: I want them to chase me. The artifact is a git-committable board-minute -- developer-shaped and self-distributing -- so I am betting that a free, honest, zero-COGS engine plus one credible launch post is enough for inbound to start, and that outbound would cheapen it.
decision: Launch inbound-only: a single MIT-licensed BYOK engine, no outbound outreach, mass adoption ahead of monetization.

prediction.statement: By 2026-09-24, with zero outbound outreach performed, the inbound-only launch will produce at least one unsolicited external pull signal -- defined as ANY of: (a) a GitHub issue or pull request opened by someone other than the author, (b) a genuine hosted-tier waitlist email to support@chu6a.dev, or (c) the repository crossing 25 stars.

seats.rationale: The artifact is git-native and developer-shaped, so it distributes itself once seen; one credible front-page post is enough of a spark, and inbound compounds from a single source. The >=1-signal bar is deliberately low because the real bet is the slope, not day-0 volume.

Inbound-only with no pre-existing audience is hope dressed as strategy. A single launch post decays inside 24h; the base rate for a solo-shipped OSS repo is silence. Without distribution you already own, 90 days of zero external engagement is the modal outcome, not the tail.

One curious developer filing one issue clears this bar, so it is closer to a coin-flip than a long shot. But note what it does NOT predict: engagement is not revenue. The hosted-tier conversion -- the bet that actually pays the bills -- is unmeasured by this minute and resolves on a far longer clock.
